title: Hearing - Sales and/or Use Tax (State,Local,MTA) - 202602012H
text:
SOAH DOCKET NO. 304-26-02369

CPA HEARING NO. 121,080

 


RE:      **************


TAXPAYER NO:  **************

AUDIT OFFICE:   **************

AUDIT PERIOD:  August 1, 2023 THROUGH July 31, 2024


Sales And Use Tax/RDT

BEFORE THE COMPTROLLER OF PUBLIC ACCOUNTS

OF THE STATE OF TEXAS


KELLY HANCOCK
Acting Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts


PETE SMITS

Representing Respondent


**************

Representing Petitioner


 



COMPTROLLER’S DECISION
 

This decision is considered final on March 2, 2026, unless a motion for rehearing is timely filed; this date of finality is calculated based on the Administrative Procedure Act (APA).[1]  The failure to timely file a motion for rehearing may result in adverse legal consequences.


Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) Kimberly LaFlair of the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH) issued a Proposal for Decision (PFD) that includes Findings of Fact and Conclusions of Law.  SOAH served the PFD on each party and each party was given an opportunity to file exceptions and replies with SOAH in accordance with SOAH’s rules of procedure.  The ALJ recommended that the Comptroller adopt the PFD as written.


After review and consideration, IT IS ORDERED that the PFD is adopted as written. 


The result from this Decision is Attachment A.  The ALJ’s recommendation letter is Attachment B.  The PFD as written is Attachment C.  Attachments A, B, and C are incorporated by reference.


Attachment A reflects a zero amount due.

 
SIGNED on this 5th day of February 2026

 

KELLY HANCOCK
Acting Comptroller of Public Accounts


By: Lisa Craven

Deputy Comptroller


Attachment A, Texas Notification of Hearing Results
Attachment B, ALJ’s recommendation letter
Attachment C, Proposal for Decision as written

 

 

 


ATTACHMENT C

 

 

SOAH Docket No. 304-26-02369                                             

Before the State Office of Administrative Hearings

TCPA Hearing No. 121,080

 

 

Proposal for Decision

 

The Tax Division (Staff) of the Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts (Comptroller) assessed ************** (Petitioner) as a successor under Texas Tax Code §§ 111.020 and 111.022 for sales and use tax owed by INDIVIDUAL A (Predecessor). Petitioner requested redetermination of the assessment, contending that it is not liable because the Predecessor agreed to pay the assessed liability. Petitioner also requested waivers of penalty and interest. In this Proposal for Decision, the Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) recommends the successor liability assessment against Petitioner be affirmed.

 

I. Notice, Jurisdiction, and Procedural History

 

Staff referred the contested case to the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH) and, on October 7, 2025, issued Petitioner a Notice of Hearing by Written Submission. On October 13, 2025, ALJ Kimberly LaFlair issued Order Setting Written Submission Hearing. Petitioner was represented by its manager, **************, and Pete Smits represented Staff. The contested case record closed on December 16, 2025.

 

There are no issues of notice or jurisdiction; therefore, those matters are set out in the Findings of Fact and Conclusions of Law without further discussion.

 

II. REASONS FOR DECISION

 

A. Evidence

 

Petitioner did not submit an exhibit list, evidentiary exhibits, or argument for the written submission hearing. Staff submitted the pleadings exchanged between the parties prior to referring the matter to SOAH and offered the following exhibits:

 


	
	Hearing Grant Letter;
	
	
	Intro Letter;
	
	
	Texas Notification of Tax/Fee Due;
	
	
	Exam;
	
	
	Business Bill of Sale;
	
	
	Application for Sales and Use Tax Permit; and
	
	
	Comptroller’s  Customer Information Control System Business Location Address Inquiry.
	


 

Staff’s exhibits were admitted without objection.

 

B. Agreed Adjustments

 

There are no agreed adjustments.

 

C. Material Facts and Issues Presented

 

Predecessor owned and operated a smoke and vape shop, BUSINESS A, located at ADDRESS, Suite ###, in CITY, Texas. Predecessor’s first taxable sale was listed as July 11, 2016, and his out-of-business date was August 31, 2024, according to Comptroller records. Predecessor filed sales and use tax returns for the period of August 1, 2023, through July 31, 2024, but did not pay the amounts due. Predecessor received jeopardy determinations for each month within that period but did not request a redetermination hearing for any of the assessments. The final jeopardy determination in the exam period, which was July 2024, was issued on September 13, 2024, and went final on October 3, 2024. [ENDNOTE 2]

 

Petitioner purchased Predecessor’s smoke and vape shop as evidenced by a Bill of Sale between it and Predecessor dated September 19, 2024. The Bill of Sale listed a sales price of $42,000, a portion of which was for goodwill and the rest for inventory. The sale included the store located at ADDRESS in CITY, Texas, “along with all its assets, shares, ownership interest, personal property, employees, leases, contracts, trademarks, copyrights, and any other tangible or intangible interest.” The Bill of Sale also stated that Predecessor “agrees to take the full liability of any taxes, unpaid invoices to vendors and not limited to bills, credits that was before the selling of the business.” Petitioner submitted a Texas Tax Registration and Application Summary on August 12, 2024, which stated that it purchased Predecessor’s business for $50,000 on August 25, 2024 Petitioner listed its first taxable sales date as September 1, 2024.

 

On December 19, 2024, Staff assessed Petitioner for sales and use tax liability, penalty, and interest in the amount of $7,881.55. Petitioner was assessed as a successor to Predecessor pursuant to Texas Tax Code Section 111.020 and 111.022 for the period of August 1, 2023, through July 31, 2024. Upon receiving the assessment, Petitioner requested redetermination, contending that Predecessor agreed to pay the assessed liability, and also requested waivers of penalty and interest. Staff did not agree to adjust the assessment and referred the matter to SOAH.

 

D. ALJ Analysis and Recommendation

 

The Comptroller is charged with the collection of taxes imposed under Title 2 of the Tax Code, which governs state taxation. See Tex. Tax Code 111.001. If a person who is liable for an amount under Title 2 of the Texas Tax Code sells the business or the stock of goods of the business or quits the business, then the successor to the seller shall withhold an amount from the purchase price sufficient to pay the amount due, unless the seller first obtains a receipt from the Comptroller showing that the amount has been paid or a certificate stating that no amount is due. Tex. Tax Code § 111.020(a), (c); 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.7(a). If the purchaser of the business or stock of goods fails to do so, the purchaser is liable for the delinquent taxes to the extent of the value of the purchase price. Tex. Tax Code § 111.020(b); 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.7(a).

 

To uphold a successor liability assessment, Staff’s evidence must first demonstrate that the predecessor’s tax liability was final and unpaid when the assessment against the successor was made. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 118,449 (2023). The assessment was made against Petitioner in December 2024. Staff’s evidence demonstrates that the last jeopardy determination in the exam period went final on October 3, 2024, and Petitioner did not dispute that the liability at issue is final and unpaid. [ENDNOTE 3]

 

In addition to the preliminary showing that the liability at issue is final and unpaid, Staff’s evidence must also establish that the predecessor (1) sold a business or stock of goods or quit the business and (2) that some amount of consideration passed to predecessor from the alleged successor. See Tex. Tax Code § 111.020(a); see also, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 111,401 (2015). When determining if a business or business assets have been sold or acquired, the Comptroller will examine the transaction to determine the parties’ intent. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.7(d).

 

It is not necessary that all the assets of a business be purchased to support a finding that a business was acquired, and a seller may have sold a business even when few assets were transferred. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 116,645 (2022). Depending on the type of business involved, a business may be sold if an owner sells: a building, land, furniture, fixtures, inventory, and the right to use the seller’s trade name; all the capital assets of a business; the name and goodwill of a business; all the inventory of a business; or fixed assets and realty necessary to operate a similar business as the seller at the same location. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.7(d).

 

In this case, there is no dispute that Petitioner purchased Predecessor’s entire business, although the exact amount of the purchase price, whether $42,000 as stated in the Bill of Sale, or $50,000, which is the sales price listed in Petitioner’s Texas Tax Registration and Application Summary, remains unclear. Therefore, the ALJ finds there is sufficient evidence to demonstrate, prima facie, that Petitioner acquired Predecessor, an entity with outstanding tax liabilities, and did not obtain a receipt from the Comptroller showing that the tax liabilities had been paid. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.7(d); see also, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 116,645 (2022).

 

While the sale of the business is not at issue, Petitioner contends that paragraph 4 in the Bill of Sale absolves it from the tax liability incurred by Predecessor. It is well established, however, that Petitioner may not contract away its liability to the detriment of the state. See Comptroller Decision Nos. 117,207 and 117,211 (2021). Nor is the Comptroller bound by a private contract to which the Comptroller is not a party. See State Tax Automated Research Document No. 201808011L (August 14, 2018); see also, Comptroller’s Decision No. 105,880 (2012). Accordingly, the ALJ finds that Petitioner is liable for Predecessor’s unpaid sales and use tax assessment as a successor pursuant to Texas Tax Code § 111.020, and its contention should be denied.

 

Late penalties are automatically imposed on delinquent taxes. Tex. Tax Code § 111.061. The Comptroller has discretionary authority to waive late penalties if a taxpayer has exercised reasonable diligence to comply with tax laws. Tex. Tax Code § 111.103. A taxpayer must establish reasonable diligence by a preponderance of the evidence. Comptroller’s Decision No. 117,926 (2022). In making the reasonable diligence determination, the Comptroller considers the taxpayer’s audit history, the tax issues involved, whether a change in Comptroller policy occurred during the audit period, whether changes in the law took effect during the audit period, the size and sophistication of the taxpayer, whether tax was collected but not remitted, whether returns were timely filed, the completeness of the taxpayer’s records, the taxpayer’s efforts to comply with the recordkeeping requirements of this state, delinquencies in other taxes, reliance on advice provided by the Comptroller’s office, and the error rate in the current audit. 34 Tex. Admin. Code §§ 3.5(b)(3), (d). To prevail, Petitioner must demonstrate, by a preponderance of the evidence, that penalty waiver is warranted. See id. § 1.26(e); see also Comptroller’s Decision No. 102,268 (2014). Other than requesting waiver, Petitioner offered no evidence or argument in support of its contention. The request for penalty waiver should be denied.

 

Petitioner also requested interest waiver. Delinquent taxes draw interest beginning 60 days after the date due. Tex. Tax Code § 111.060(c). The Comptroller has discretionary authority to waive interest assessments and may exercise his discretion if interest was imposed as a result of undue delay caused by Comptroller personnel, reliance on advice provided by the Comptroller’s office, or natural disaster. Id. § 111.103; 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.5(e). To prevail, Petitioner must demonstrate, by a preponderance of the evidence, that interest waiver is warranted. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e); see also Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 102,268 and 109,069 (2014). Petitioner did not demonstrate any of the factors that warrant interest waiver. The contention should be denied.

 

III. Findings of Fact


	
	The Tax Division (Staff) of the Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts (Comptroller) assessed ************** (Petitioner) as a successor under Texas Tax Code §§ 111.020 and 111.022 for sales and use tax owed by INDIVIDUAL A (Predecessor).
	
	
	Predecessor owned and operated a smoke and vape shop, BUSINESS A, located at ADDRESS, Suite ###, in CITY, Texas.
	
	
	Predecessor’s first taxable sale was listed as July 11, 2016, and his out of business date was August 31, 2024, according to Comptroller records.
	
	
	Predecessor filed sales and use tax returns for the period of August 1, 2023, through July 31, 2024, but did not pay the amounts due.
	
	
	Predecessor received jeopardy determinations for each month within that period but did not request a redetermination hearing for any of the assessments.
	
	
	The final jeopardy determination in the exam period, which was July 2024, was issued on September 13, 2024, and went final on October 3, 2024.
	
	
	Petitioner purchased Predecessor’s smoke and vape shop as evidenced by a Bill of Sale between it and Predecessor dated September 19, 2024.
	
	
	The Bill of Sale listed a sales price of $42,000, a portion of which was for goodwill and the rest for inventory.
	
	
	The sale included the store located at ADDRESS in CITY, Texas, “along with all its assets, shares, ownership interest, personal property, employees, leases, contracts, trademarks, copyrights, and any other tangible or intangible interest.”
	
	
	The Bill of Sale also stated that Predecessor “agrees to take the full liability of any taxes, unpaid invoices to vendors and not limited to bills, credits that was before the selling of the business.”
	
	
	Petitioner submitted a Texas Tax Registration and Application Summary on August 12, 2024, which stated that it purchased Predecessor’s business for $50,000 on August 25, 2024. Petitioner listed its first taxable sales date as September 1, 2024.
	
	
	On December 19, 2024, Staff assessed Petitioner for sales and use tax liability, penalty, and interest in the amount of $7,881.55.
	
	
	Petitioner was assessed as a successor to Predecessor pursuant to Texas Tax Code Section 111.020 and 111.022 for the period of August 1, 2023, through July 31, 2024.
	
	
	Petitioner requested redetermination.
	
	
	Staff did not agree to adjust the assessment and referred the matter to the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH).
	
	
	On October 7, 2025, Staff issued a Notice of Hearing by Written Submission. The notice contained a statement of the nature of the hearing; a statement of the legal authority and jurisdiction under which the hearing was to be held; a reference to the particular sections of the statutes and rules involved; and a short, plain statement of the factual matters asserted, or an attachment that incorporated by reference the factual matters asserted in the complaint or petition filed with the state agency.
	
	
	On October 13, 2025, Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) Kimberly LaFlair issued an order setting the written submission hearing.
	
	
	Petitioner did not submit an exhibit list, evidentiary exhibits, or argument for the written submission hearing.
	
	
	The contested case record closed on December 16, 2025.
	


 

IV. Conclusions of Law


	
	The Comptroller has jurisdiction over this matter. See Tex. Tax Code ch. 111.
	
	
	SOAH has jurisdiction over matters related to the hearing in this matter, including the authority to issue a proposal for decision with findings of fact and conclusions of law. See Tex. Gov’t Code ch. 2003.
	
	
	Staff provided proper and timely notice of the hearing. See Tex. Gov’t Code ch. 2001; Tex. Tax Code § 111.009.
	
	
	The Comptroller is charged with the collection of taxes imposed under Title 2 of the Tax Code, which governs state taxation. See Tex. Tax Code 111.001.
	
	
	If a person who is liable for an amount under Title 2 of the Texas Tax Code sells the business or the stock of goods of the business or quits the business, then the successor to the seller shall withhold an amount from the purchase price sufficient to pay the amount due, unless the seller first obtains a receipt from the Comptroller showing that the amount has been paid or a certificate stating that no amount is due. Tex. Tax Code § 111.020(a), (c); 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.7(a).
	
	
	If the purchaser of the business or stock of goods fails to do so, the purchaser is liable for the delinquent taxes to the extent of the value of the purchase price. Tex. Tax Code § 111.020(b); 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.7(a).
	
	
	To uphold a successor liability assessment, Staff’s evidence must first demonstrate that the predecessor’s tax liability was final and unpaid when the assessment against the successor was made. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 118,449 (2023).
	
	
	The ALJ concludes Staff’s evidence supports the conclusion that the liability at issue was final and unpaid when the assessment against Petitioner was made.
	
	
	In addition to the preliminary showing that the liability at issue is final and unpaid, Staff’s evidence must also establish that the predecessor (1) sold a business or stock of goods or quit the business and (2) that some amount of consideration passed to predecessor from the alleged successor. See Tex. Tax Code § 111.020(a); see also, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 111,401 (2015).
	
	
	When determining if a business or business assets have been sold or acquired, the Comptroller will examine the transaction to determine the parties’ intent. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.7(d).
	
	
	It is not necessary that all the assets of a business be purchased to support a finding that a business was acquired, and a seller may have sold a business even when few assets were transferred. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 116,645 (2022).
	
	
	Depending on the type of business involved, a business may be sold if an owner sells: a building, land, furniture, fixtures, inventory, and the right to use the seller’s trade name; all the capital assets of a business; the name and goodwill of a business; all the inventory of a business; or fixed assets and realty necessary to operate a similar business as the seller at the same location. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.7(d).
	
	
	The ALJ finds there is sufficient evidence to demonstrate, prima facie, that Petitioner acquired Predecessor, an entity with outstanding tax liabilities, and did not obtain a receipt from the Comptroller showing that the tax liabilities had been paid. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.7(d); see also, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 116,645 (2022).
	
	
	Petitioner may not contract away its liability to the detriment of the state. See Comptroller Decision Nos. 117,207 and 117,211 (2021).
	
	
	The Comptroller is not bound by a private contract to which the Comptroller is not a party. See State Tax Automated Research Document No. 201808011L (August 14, 2018); see also, Comptroller’s Decision No. 105,880 (2012).
	
	
	The ALJ finds that Petitioner is liable for Predecessor’s unpaid sales and use tax assessment as a successor pursuant to Texas Tax Code § 111.020 and recommends its contention be denied.
	
	
	Late penalties are automatically imposed on delinquent taxes. Tex. Tax Code § 111.061.
	
	
	The Comptroller has discretionary authority to waive late penalties if a taxpayer has exercised reasonable diligence to comply with tax laws. Tex. Tax Code § 111.103.
	
	
	A taxpayer must establish reasonable diligence by a preponderance of the evidence. Comptroller’s Decision No. 117,926 (2022).
	
	
	In making the reasonable diligence determination, the Comptroller considers the taxpayer’s audit history, the tax issues involved, whether a change in Comptroller policy occurred during the audit period, whether changes in the law took effect during the audit period, the size and sophistication of the taxpayer, whether tax was collected but not remitted, whether returns were timely filed, the completeness of the taxpayer’s records, the taxpayer’s efforts to comply with the recordkeeping requirements of this state, delinquencies in other taxes, reliance on advice provided by the Comptroller’s office, and the error rate in the current audit. 34 Tex. Admin. Code §§ 3.5(b)(3), (d).
	
	
	To prevail, Petitioner must demonstrate, by a preponderance of the evidence, that penalty waiver is warranted. See Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e); see also Comptroller’s Decision No. 102,268 (2014).
	
	
	Petitioner offered no evidence or argument in support of its contention, and the request for penalty waiver should be denied.
	
	
	Delinquent taxes draw interest beginning 60 days after the date due. Tex. Tax Code § 111.060(c).
	
	
	The Comptroller has discretionary authority to waive interest assessments and may exercise his discretion if interest was imposed as a result of undue delay caused by Comptroller personnel, reliance on advice provided by the Comptroller’s office, or natural disaster. Tex. Tax Code § 111.103; 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.5(e).
	
	
	To prevail, Petitioner must demonstrate, by a preponderance of the evidence, that interest waiver is warranted. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e); see also Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 102,268 and 109,069 (2014).
	
	
	Petitioner did not demonstrate any of the factors that warrant interest waiver, and the contention should be denied.
	


 

Signed December 18, 2025.

 

Kimberly LaFlair

Presiding Administrative Law Judge

 

 


 




ENDNOTE




[1] The date calculated is 25 days after this decision is signed. See APA, Tex. Gov’t Code § 2001.146(a); S.B. 1095, Acts 2017, 85th Leg.   For additional guidance, refer to the Frequently Asked Questions Related to Motions for Rehearing, found here: http://comptroller.texas.gov/taxes/publications/96-1789.pdf 

[2] The dates that each assessment during the exam period went final are as follows: November 2, 2023; December 7, 2023; January 4, 2024; February 1, 2024; March 7, 2024; April 4, 2024; April 23, 2024; June 6, 2024; June 12, 2024; July 29, 2024; August 27, 2024; and October 3, 2024. 

[3] In the Notice of Hearing, it references that Petitioner disagreed with the Position Letter and attached a Tax Payment Receipt for $3,000.00 that had already been credited to Petitioner’s account. Other than this statement, there is no evidence in the record of this payment.
subjects: CONTRACT/WRITTEN AGREEMENT BETWEEN TWO PRIVATE PARTIES-STATE NOT BOUND BY CONTRACTUAL PROVISIONS THAT CAN SHIFT SALES TAX RESPONSIBILITIES
STATE OF TEXAS-NOT PARTY TO CONTRACT BETWEEN PRIVATE PARTIES
SUCCESSOR LIABILITY-TAX DIVISION MUST PROVE (1) SALE OF BUSINESS OR STOCK OF GOODS OCCURRED (2) VALUE OF PURCHASE PRICE (3) BUYER DID NOT OBTAIN  CERTIFICATE OF NO TAX DUE
TAXPAYER MAY NOT CONTRACT AWAY ITS TAX LIABILITY TO THE DETRIMENT OF THE STATE
